title: The Skin That Remembers
content:
The woman arrived at midnight, which should have been the first warning.

Dr. Fang Wei recognized the type immediately. Late forties, expensive handbag, clothes that cost more than his first car. The kind of woman who'd spent twenty years and a fortune maintaining the illusion of thirty-five. She sat in his consultation room with her back straight and her hands folded, the way his mother used to sit through dinner parties while his father drank himself stupid.

"Your receptionist said you don't take new patients," she said. Her voice had that particular Shanghai crispness, the accent of old money and newer secrets. "I told her you'd make an exception."

Fang leaned back in his leather chair. The clinic was quiet at this hour. His last real patient had left at nine. The cleaning staff had finished at ten. Now only the night lights hummed in the corridors, casting everything in a sterile blue glow.

"I don't make exceptions," he said.

She reached into her bag and placed a photograph on his desk. A younger woman, early twenties maybe, with sharp cheekbones and full lips. Beautiful in that manufactured way that Chinese entertainment companies spent millions to replicate.

"That was me," she said. "Ten years ago."

Fang picked up the photograph. The woman in the image had something the woman in his office didn't. Not just youth. Something underneath it. A kind of glow that even the best lighting couldn't fake.

"I've been to Seoul three times," she continued. "Tokyo twice. Los Angeles once. I've had everything injected, lifted, lasered, and scraped. And still, every morning, I look in the mirror and see the same thing." She touched her face. "This. Coming apart."

He put the photograph down. "Mrs. Lin—"

"I know what you do."

The words hung in the air between them. Fang didn't move. Didn't blink.

"I don't know what you mean," he said carefully.

She smiled. It was not a pleasant expression. "My sister came to you two years ago. When she left your clinic, she looked twenty-five again. Her husband thought she'd found a miracle. Her friends thought she'd found a better salon." The smile faded. "Six months later, she jumped off the Shanghai Tower."

Fang remembered the case. The news had been quiet about it. One of those human interest pieces that ran for a day before disappearing into the endless churn of Chinese media. A wealthy socialite, depression, tragedy. No one had asked questions.

"I'm sorry for your loss," he said. "But I'm a cosmetic surgeon. I perform standard procedures. If your sister suffered from mental health issues—"

"Her face was peeling off at night."

The words cut through his prepared response like a blade through paper.

"The skin," Mrs. Lin continued. "It would come off in sheets while she slept. She'd wake up with patches of raw muscle showing through. She tried to hide it with makeup, but it kept getting worse. She went to dermatologists, psychiatrists, even a shaman. Nothing worked." She leaned forward. "Nothing except coming back here. Which she did. Four times. Each time, you fixed it. And each time, it cost her more."

Fang's palms were sweating. He kept them flat on the desk.

"Mrs. Lin, I think you should leave."

"I'm not here to expose you." She opened her bag again and pulled out a folder thick with documents. "I'm here to make you an offer. I know what you use. I know where it comes from. And I know you're running out of time."

"What do you want?"

"Save me." Her voice cracked for the first time. "I don't care about the cost. I don't care about the consequences. I saw my sister's face when she left your clinic that first time. She was happy. Really, truly happy. I want that. Just once before I die."

Fang looked at her for a long moment. Then he opened his desk drawer and pulled out a key.

"Follow me."

---

The procedure room was hidden behind the main operating theater. A false wall, a keypad lock, and a door that looked like a supply closet. Inside, the air was cold and dry. Shelves lined the walls, filled with jars.

Mrs. Lin stood in the center of the room, her arms wrapped around herself. "What is this place?"

"The real clinic." Fang moved to a refrigerated cabinet and unlocked it. Inside, stacked like expensive handbags, were the skins. Dozens of them. Each one preserved in a special solution, each one labeled with a date and a name.

He had discovered the process three years ago, buried in a medical text from the Qing Dynasty. The book had been hidden in a private collection in Hong Kong, sold to him by a dealer who didn't know what he had. The original author had been a Taoist physician who claimed to have learned the technique from a spirit. The text was written in a dialect so old that Fang had needed a scholar from Beijing to translate it.

The scholar had disappeared three weeks after completing the work. Fang had paid his family a generous sum and thought nothing of it.

"The skin comes from spirits," he said, selecting a jar from the third shelf. "Bound spirits. Spirits who've been trapped between worlds, denied their passage to the afterlife. Their bodies exist in a state between flesh and energy. When properly treated, the skin can be grafted onto a living human."

Mrs. Lin's face had gone pale. "That's... that's monstrous."

"Is it?" Fang held up the jar. The skin inside moved, shifting against the glass like a living thing. "These spirits are already dead. Their bodies are useless to them. I'm giving their remains purpose. I'm making people beautiful."

"You're making people into monsters."

"I'm giving people what they want." He set the jar on the counter. "The procedure takes three hours. You'll be under general anesthesia. When you wake up, the face you see in the mirror will be the face of your memories. The face of your best self. For the first six months, it will feel like you've been reborn."

"And after six months?"

Fang didn't answer.

"Tell me."

"The spirit begins to remember itself." He spoke the words flatly, the way a doctor delivers a terminal diagnosis. "The skin has a kind of memory. A cellular identity. Over time, it tries to reconnect with the spirit's original form. This causes... complications."

"Complications like my sister's face rotting off."

"Yes."

"And there's no cure?"

"There's a treatment." Fang opened the jar. The smell was not unpleasant—like ozone and rain on hot pavement. "The spirit must be fed. Small amounts of the patient's own life force, channeled through regular sessions. It's a maintenance process. The patient continues to look younger, but the debt accumulates."

"What happens when the debt is due?"

He had never told anyone this part. Not even the woman from the antique shop who'd sold him the text. But Mrs. Lin's eyes held a terrible understanding. She already knew.

"The spirit takes the soul," he said. "The patient becomes the spirit. Their consciousness dissolves into the skin, and the spirit walks free in their body."

Mrs. Lin sat down heavily on the procedure table. She was silent for a long time.

"How many?" she finally asked.

"How many what?"

"How many have you done this to?"

"Thirty-seven," Fang said. "Including your sister."

"And how many are still alive?"

"Twelve. The rest have... transitioned."

"Transitioned." She laughed, a hollow sound that echoed off the cold walls. "You mean they're dead. Their souls are dead, and their bodies are running around Shanghai wearing the faces of monsters."

"I prefer to think of it as reincarnation."

"You're a monster."

"I'm a businessman." Fang placed the jar in the preparation tray. "Now. Do you want the procedure or not?"

Mrs. Lin looked at the jar. The skin inside had stopped moving. It was waiting, the way a predator waits, patient and aware.

"How long do I have?"

"Six months of perfect beauty. Then diminishing returns. If you follow the treatment schedule, you might get two years before the debt comes due."

"And if I don't follow the treatment?"

"Then the skin will take what it's owed anyway. But it will be less... gentle."

Mrs. Lin stood. She walked to the jar and placed her palm against the glass. Inside, the skin pressed back, flattening against her hand as if trying to touch her through the barrier.

"Do it," she said.

Fang nodded and began preparing the instruments.

---

Three hours later, Mrs. Lin left the clinic wearing a stranger's face. Her skin was smooth. Her eyes were bright. She looked like the photograph she'd shown him, and for a moment, Fang felt something almost like satisfaction.

He locked the hidden room behind her and went to his office. The security monitors showed the hallway empty. The street outside was quiet. Shanghai slept, unaware of what happened in the basement of the sleek glass building on Huaihai Road.

Fang sat at his desk and poured himself a glass of whiskey. The ice clinked against the crystal as he raised it to his lips.

His phone buzzed.

A message from an unknown number: *The Taoist is in the city. He knows about the skins.*

Fang stared at the message. Then he deleted it, finished his whiskey, and went home to his empty apartment.

In the morning, the woman who had been Mrs. Lin would wake up and see her new face in the mirror. She would smile at her reflection, and the reflection would smile back.

But the reflection's smile would be a fraction of a second too slow.

And somewhere in the old city, a man in worn robes would be unpacking a bag of talismans and iron nails, preparing for a hunt.

title: Chapter 2
content:
# Chapter 2: The Taoist at Dawn

The needle slipped.

Dr. Fang watched the blood bead form on Mrs. Chen's temple, a perfect ruby against beige foundation. His hand had never trembled in thirty years. Not during the war. Not during his first solo surgery. Not when he'd cut that first spirit skin from a wailing ghost in his basement.

But the needle had slipped.

"Sorry," he said, pressing a cotton ball to the tiny wound. "Didn't sleep well."

Mrs. Chen's eyes were fixed on the mirror. She didn't feel the prick. She never did. They never did, three days after the procedure. The nerves were still regrowing.

"It's remarkable, Doctor." She touched her cheek, the skin there smooth as river stone. "I look like I did at twenty-two."

You look better, Fang thought. At twenty-two, she'd had acne scars and crow's feet from too much sun. Now her face was porcelain. Flawless. Empty.

He finished the injection — a simple filler, nothing supernatural — and peeled off his gloves. The morning light cut through his office blinds, striping the floor in gold. Outside, Shanghai was waking up. Delivery scooters whined through wet streets. A billion people starting their day.

None of them knew what he kept in the cold room three floors below.

"Come back Thursday for the final check," he said. "The swelling will go down by then."

Mrs. Chen floated out on a cloud of gratitude. Fang watched her go, noting the way her reflection in the hallway mirror seemed to lag behind her body by half a second. He'd installed special mirrors throughout the clinic. Silver-backed, with iron filings worked into the glass. Taoist standard issue, circa 1987, available on Alibaba for two thousand yuan.

The mirrors didn't stop the spirits. Nothing stopped the spirits. But the mirrors showed him which patients were still human.

Mrs. Chen's reflection caught up with her just before the elevator doors closed.

Fang let out a breath he'd been holding since dawn.

---

The first patient of the day was never the problem. It was the waiting room that worried him. The waiting room, and the old man who'd been sitting in it since six AM.

Fang checked the security feed on his phone. The man was thin, ancient, dressed in a gray Mao suit that had gone shiny at the elbows. He sat perfectly still, hands on his knees, watching the door like a cat watches a mouse hole. No phone. No book. No water. Just waiting.

The nurses had tried to take his name three times. He'd smiled at each of them, showing teeth stained brown by decades of tea, and said nothing.

Fang's assistant Li Wei appeared in his doorway. "Doctor, your eight o'clock is here. Should I bring her back?"

"What about the old man?"

"He refuses to give his name. Says he'll see you when you're ready."

Fang's right hand twitched. The one that had slipped with the needle.

"Let him wait."

---

The morning passed in a blur of consultations and minor procedures. Fang's days were methodical. Fillers and Botox for the wealthy wives of Pudong. Laser treatments for socialites who'd spent too long in the Maldives. And one new consultation — a woman named Zhao Liling, forty-four, who wanted the full transformation.

The special one.

He saved those consultations for the end of the day, when the clinic was quiet and his hands were steady.

Zhao Liling sat across from him, her hands clasped in her lap. She was beautiful in the way all his patients were beautiful — carefully constructed, impeccably maintained, held together by discipline and expensive creams. But her eyes were tired. The eyes of a woman who'd spent twenty years pretending she wasn't getting older.

"Dr. Fang," she said, "my husband left me for a girl from his office. She's twenty-eight. He says he wants someone who matches his energy."

Fang nodded. He'd heard this story before. He'd heard every variation.

"I want to look like I did when we got married. Can you do that?"

"I can do better."

He showed her photos. Before and after shots of women who'd come to him with sagging jaws and hollow cheeks, and left with faces so tight they seemed to glow. The photos were real. The results were real. The method was not.

"How does it work?" she asked.

Fang smiled. "It's a proprietary technique. Stem cells from the patient's own fat tissue, combined with growth factors and a special collagen matrix. The recovery period is three days. After that, you'll look fifteen years younger. Minimum."

The lie was smooth. Perfect. He'd been telling it for seven years.

"What's the catch?"

"Price. One million yuan. Half upfront, half upon completion."

She didn't flinch. She wrote a check on the spot, her hand steady.

Fang took it, his own hand still trembling slightly.

---

The old man was still in the waiting room when Fang left at seven.

The nurses had gone home. The reception desk was dark. But the old man sat in the same chair, same position, hands on knees, watching the door.

Fang locked the front entrance and walked past him.

"You have a cold room, Doctor."

Fang stopped.

"Third floor below the clinic. Kept at four degrees Celsius. Perfect for biological samples."

Fang turned. The old man hadn't moved. His eyes were black in the dim light, black as wet stones.

"I'm a medical professional," Fang said. "Biological samples are part of the job."

"Yes." The old man stood, his joints cracking like dry wood. "But most doctors keep their samples in containers. You keep yours in jars. Glass jars. With iron lids."

Fang's heart stopped. Just for a second. Just long enough for him to feel the cold spreading from his chest.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Your wife knows." The old man walked toward him, his steps soft on the marble floor. "She found one, didn't she? Three years ago. A jar in the freezer, labeled 'steamed dumplings.' She opened it."

Fang remembered that night. The screaming. The police. The way his wife had looked at him, her face pale as bone, as she held up the jar containing a woman's face.

He'd told them it was a silicone model. Teaching purposes. The police had believed him. His wife had not.

"She doesn't live here anymore," Fang said.

"No. She lives in Hangzhou, with her mother. She's afraid of you, Doctor. But she's more afraid of what you keep in the basement."

The old man was close now. Close enough for Fang to smell him — tea, incense, and something else. Something metallic.

"Who are you?"

"My name is Zhang Wei. I'm a Taoist priest. I've been tracking you for six months."

Fang laughed. He couldn't help it. The absurdity of it — a Taoist priest in a Mao suit, tracking him like some kind of monster.

"You think I'm a demon?"

"No." The old man's eyes didn't blink. "I think you're a man who made a deal. I think you found a way to harvest the skin of the dead and use it to make the living young. I think you've done it hundreds of times. And I think the spirits you harvested want their skins back."

Fang's laughter died.

"The first one will come tonight," the old man said. "She's been waiting for three years. Her skin is on a woman named Chen Li Hua. A woman you treated in March."

Mrs. Chen. The first patient of the day.

"The skin remembers, Doctor. It remembers who it belonged to. And when the spirit wants it back, she'll take it by force."

The old man reached into his pocket. Fang flinched, expecting a weapon. But Zhang Wei pulled out a talisman, yellow paper with red characters painted in what looked like blood.

"This will protect you. For tonight."

"Why would you help me?"

The old man smiled. His teeth were brown, but his eyes were kind.

"Because you're not evil, Dr. Fang. You're just a man who made a terrible mistake. And if the spirits take you, they'll keep taking. They'll take your patients, your nurses, your security guards. A hundred innocent people will die because you couldn't leave well enough alone."

The talisman hung in the air between them.

Fang took it.

"Don't lose it," the old man said. "And don't let her touch your skin. If she touches your skin, she'll wear your face next."

He walked away, his footsteps echoing in the empty lobby.

Fang stood alone, the talisman burning in his hand, and tried to remember how to breathe.

---

He went home to his apartment in the Jing'an district. Seventeenth floor. Floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the city. A kitchen he never used and a bedroom he never slept in.

He spent most nights in the cold room.

But tonight, he sat on his leather couch, the talisman in his lap, and watched the security feed from the clinic.

The cameras showed empty hallways. Silent rooms. The cold room, with its rows of jars, each one containing a face suspended in clear fluid.

He'd counted them this morning. Two hundred and forty-three jars. Two hundred and forty-three spirits whose skin he'd stolen.

Two hundred and forty-three women who wanted their faces back.

The clock on his wall ticked past midnight.

The lights in the clinic flickered.

Fang leaned forward, his eyes fixed on the screen.

A woman appeared in the hallway. She was naked. Her body was smooth, featureless, like a mannequin. No hair. No nails. No face.

She walked toward the cold room.

Fang's phone rang.

He answered without looking at the caller ID.

"Doctor." The old man's voice was calm. "She's inside."

"I see her."

"Don't go to the clinic. No matter what you see, don't go."

The woman stopped in front of the cold room door. She raised her hand — her smooth, featureless hand — and pressed it against the glass.

The door unlocked.

Fang's blood turned to ice.

"I didn't do that," he whispered.

"I know." The old man sighed. "She's been inside before. She's been inside a hundred times. She knows every lock, every code, every weakness."

The woman entered the cold room. The camera showed her walking past the jars, her head turning as if she were looking for something.

She stopped in front of Mrs. Chen's jar.

The jar labeled "Chen Li Hua, March 15."

She reached through the glass.

Fang watched, his mouth open, as her hand passed through the jar like it was made of smoke. She pulled out the face — the beautiful, perfect face he'd harvested from a spirit in the Huangpu River — and held it up.

Then she turned to the camera.

And she put the face on.

It wasn't beautiful. It wasn't perfect. The skin stretched wrong, the eyes too wide, the mouth too large. It was a mask worn by something that didn't understand human anatomy.

She smiled.

Her teeth were Mrs. Chen's teeth.

"I want my skin back, Doctor," she said.

Her voice came through the phone and the camera at the same time, a stereo whisper that made the hair on Fang's arms stand up.

The talisman in his lap burst into flame.

Fang dropped it, stamping out the fire with his bare feet. When he looked up, the security feed showed the cold room empty.

The woman was gone.

But the door to her jar was open.

And on his phone, the call was still connected.

"Doctor." The old man's voice

title: The Taoist Comes Calling
content:
The first thing Dr. Fang noticed was the silence.

The Puxi clinic always hummed. Air conditioning units rattled. Reception phones buzzed. Nurses' heels clicked across marble floors. But at 11:47 PM, with the last patient gone and the sterilization cycle running in the back, silence pressed against the walls like something alive.

Fang sat in his private office, reviewing next week's surgical schedule. Three face lifts. Two rhinoplasties. One full reconstruction. His fingers moved across the tablet screen, but his mind kept drifting to the news report he'd watched at dinner.

*Body of missing woman found in Huangpu District. Facial skin entirely removed. Authorities suspect—*

He'd turned it off before they could say what they suspected.

The call bell rang.

Fang looked up. The front door was locked. Patients needed appointments. Walk-ins weren't possible after hours unless they had his private number, and no one had called.

The bell rang again.

He set down the tablet. Through the glass partition, he could see the reception area—empty, dimly lit, the potted bamboo casting long shadows against the wall. A figure stood at the door.

Fang checked his phone. No messages. No missed calls.

He walked to the front. The man outside wore a simple gray changshan, traditional-style clothing that looked out of place in Lujiazui's glass towers. Old. Worn. The kind of thing you'd see on monks in the countryside, not businessmen in Shanghai.

Fang stopped at the door. "We're closed. Come back tomorrow."

The man didn't move. His face was weathered, deep lines carved around his eyes like riverbeds. But his eyes themselves—black, sharp, seeing too much.

"I'm not here for a consultation, Dr. Fang."

The voice carried through the glass. Low. Calm. The kind of voice that didn't need to raise to be heard.

"You know my name."

"I know many things." The man pressed a palm against the glass. The door wasn't locked. Fang always kept it locked. But the latch clicked open anyway, and the door swung inward.

Fang stepped back.

The man entered. His cloth shoes made no sound on the marble. He stopped in the center of the reception area, turning slowly, taking in the space—the framed certificates, the patient photos, the tasteful modern art.

"You have a beautiful clinic. Very clean. Very sterile." He inhaled deeply. "But I can smell them."

"Smell who?"

"The dead." The man's eyes found Fang's. "You've been busy, Doctor. Your hands have touched what they shouldn't."

Fang's jaw tightened. "I don't know what you're talking about. I'm a cosmetic surgeon. I help people feel beautiful. If you're not here for a procedure, I'll have to ask you to leave."

"Beautiful." The man laughed, soft and dry. "You strip skin from the wandering dead. You stretch it over living faces. You call that beauty?"

"You're insane."

"Am I?" The man reached into his sleeve. Fang's heart seized—but the hand emerged with nothing more than a small bronze coin, old and green with age. "I've been following a trail, Dr. Fang. Three weeks ago, a woman in Pudong. She went to sleep looking fifty-two. She woke up looking twenty-five. Her husband was thrilled. Her friends were jealous. But last Tuesday, her face began to rot."

Fang said nothing.

"The skin rejected her blood. Black veins spread from her cheeks to her neck. She's in a hospital now, sedated, while the doctors try to understand why her flesh is dying from the outside in." The man held up the coin. "But I understand. That skin wasn't hers. It belonged to someone else. And the original owner wants it back."

"She had an allergic reaction. It happens."

"Does it?" The man stepped closer. "I've seen seven cases in the last month. Seven women who underwent your 'Revive' procedure. Seven women whose new faces are now decaying faster than their old ones ever did. And in each case, I find the same thing."

He flipped the coin. It caught the dim light, revealing a symbol etched into the bronze—a trigram, old and powerful.

"The spirits know when their skin is used. They feel it stretching across living bones. They taste the blood of the women who wear them. And they're gathering, Dr. Fang. They're remembering who they were. They're coming to reclaim what you stole."

Fang's phone buzzed. He glanced at it. A message from a blocked number.

*She's awake.*

"I don't know who you are," Fang said, keeping his voice steady. "But you need to leave. Now. Or I'll call the police."

"Call them." The man smiled, showing teeth that were too white for his weathered face. "Tell them a Taoist priest is harassing you. Tell them I'm making wild accusations about ghost skin and stolen flesh. I'm sure they'll take you very seriously."

He tucked the coin back into his sleeve.

"But before I go—a warning. You think you're in control. You think because you harvest from the dead, you're safe from the dead. But the dead don't forget, Doctor. And they don't forgive."

The man turned and walked to the door. He paused with his hand on the frame.

"One of your patients died tonight. The first one. The one you treated six months ago. Her face sloughed off in her sleep. Her husband found her in the morning with nothing but muscle and bone where her features used to be."

Fang's blood went cold.

"She was the first," the Taoist continued. "But she won't be the last. And the spirit whose skin she wore—that spirit is very, very angry."

He stepped out into the night. The door swung shut behind him, latch clicking into place.

Fang stood alone in the silence.

His phone buzzed again.

*She's awake. She's asking for you.*

He read the message three times. Then he deleted it, grabbed his coat, and locked up the clinic.

The drive to the storage facility took forty minutes. Fang kept checking his rearview mirror, expecting headlights, expecting the gray-clad figure to appear from nowhere. But the roads were empty, and the facility's security guard barely looked up as Fang swiped his card through the reader.

Unit 47. Climate controlled. Biometric lock.

Fang pressed his thumb to the scanner. The door hissed open.

Inside, the air was cold and still. Racks lined the walls, each containing clear cryogenic containers. Labels with dates and names. Patient codes. Batch numbers.

Seventy-two units. Seventy-two skins. Harvested from seventy-two spirits over the last eighteen months.

Fang walked to the back, where a newer unit sat separate from the rest. This one wasn't labeled. It wasn't in the system. It was his private stock, his backup plan, his insurance.

He opened the lid.

The face inside was perfect.

Young. Beautiful. Skin like porcelain. Features arranged in that impossible symmetry that made men turn their heads and women burn with envy.

He'd harvested it three nights ago. The spirit had been fresh, newly dead, still clinging to its mortal form. He'd worked quickly, precisely, removing the skin in one continuous sheet. The spirit had screamed, of course. They always screamed. But the sound didn't travel through the concrete walls, and by morning, the screaming had stopped.

Fang touched the cold surface. The skin felt alive, almost warm, like it was waiting.

"I'm not done yet," he whispered. "I still need more."

Behind him, the door clicked.

Fang spun around.

The storage unit was empty. The door was closed. The biometric lock showed green—sealed, secure.

But something had changed.

The air was warmer. The cryogenic containers hummed, but their frosted surfaces were clear, no condensation. And the temperature gauge on the wall—

Forty degrees Celsius.

Impossible. The system was set to negative twenty.

Fang reached for his phone. The screen was black. Dead.

He tried the door. The biometric lock was unresponsive. He slammed his palm against it, again and again, but the mechanism didn't budge.

A sound came from behind him.

Wet. Sliding.

He turned.

The cryogenic containers were opening. Not all at once, but one by one, their lids lifting on invisible hinges. Mist poured out, thick and cold, filling the room until Fang could barely see.

And in the mist, shapes moved.

Human shapes. Or what had once been human.

They had no faces. Just smooth, featureless expanses where eyes and mouths and noses should have been. But they had hands—cold, pale hands that reached for him from all directions.

The first one touched his arm.

Fang screamed.

The hand was ice. Not cold—ice. Frost spread across his sleeve, crept up his arm, numbed his skin. He tried to pull away, but the hand held fast, and more hands found him—his shoulders, his neck, his face.

They weren't attacking him.

They were searching.

Fingers pressed against his cheeks, his forehead, his jaw. They traced the contours of his face, mapping every ridge and hollow. And as they touched, he felt something beneath his skin begin to shift.

His face was moving.

No—his face was being *remembered.*

The faceless spirits pressed closer, and Fang understood.

They didn't want their skins back.

They wanted to take his.

title: The Collection Opens
content:
The basement air tasted like copper and formaldehyde.

Dr. Fang pressed his palm flat against the cold metal door, feeling the vibration of the city above him. Ten floors of sterile clinic. Fifty meters of elevator shaft. And beneath it all, his true work.

The lock clicked. The door swung inward.

His collection waited in the dark.

Rows of glass jars lined the walls, each one filled with a pale amber solution. Some held complete faces, preserved like masks suspended in honey. Others contained smaller pieces—a perfect nose bridge, high cheekbones, the delicate curve of a jawline. Each one had cost him something. A surgical fee. A patient's trust. A spirit's final scream.

He walked past the first three rows without stopping. These were old stock. Inferior. The skin had been harvested too quickly, without proper ritual preparation. The women had aged again within months, their faces collapsing like deflating balloons. He'd learned from those mistakes.

The fourth row held his masterpieces.

He stopped at jar number forty-seven. Inside, a face floated in perfect stillness. Heart-shaped. Porcelain pale. Lips that curved into a gentle pout even in death. The woman had been twenty-three when she came to him, crying about the fine lines around her eyes. He'd promised her a solution. He'd given her one.

The face was still flawless six months later. Her skin hadn't rotted. It hadn't rejected the transplant. It hadn't done anything except make her the most beautiful woman in any room she entered.

He smiled.

"Still holding up, Lin?"

The face did not respond. It never did. That was the point.

His phone buzzed in his pocket. He pulled it out, squinting at the screen.

*Dr. Fang, I need to see you. Urgent. It's about my face. - Mrs. Chen*

He frowned. Mrs. Chen was a twenty-year patient. Loyal. Wealthy. Discreet. She'd had her first transplant five years ago, and the results had been exceptional. No complications. No rejection. She'd referred him a dozen new clients since then.

He typed back: *What seems to be the problem?*

The reply came in seconds: *I can't explain. Just come. Please.*

He pocketed the phone and took one last look at his collection. The jars gleamed in the dim light, row after row of perfect faces waiting for their second chance at life. He'd built this empire from nothing. From a tiny clinic in a run-down building to a penthouse suite in the most expensive district in Shanghai. From a nobody to the most sought-after cosmetic surgeon in the city.

And now someone was threatening to tear it all apart.

The Taoist had been asking questions. Old Man Wang from the herbal shop on Nanjing Road had called to warn him. "She's been poking around," he'd said, his voice a dry rasp. "Asking about unusual procedures. About skin grafts. About ghosts."

Fang had laughed it off. "Superstitious nonsense."

But he'd installed the basement lock that same day.

He turned off the lights and climbed back up the stairs, sealing the door behind him. The elevator ride to the ground floor felt longer than usual. He checked his phone again. Mrs. Chen had sent another message: *Please hurry. I'm in the VIP consultation room.*

The lobby was empty at this hour. The receptionist had gone home. The cleaning crew wouldn't arrive until midnight. He walked through the marble-floored hallway, his footsteps echoing off the walls, until he reached the VIP suite.

He knocked twice. "Mrs. Chen?"

The door opened a crack. A single eye peered out at him, bloodshot and wild. "Get in. Quickly."

He slipped through the gap, and the door slammed shut behind him.

The room was dim, lit only by a single lamp. Mrs. Chen stood with her back to him, her shoulders shaking. She wore a silk robe that she'd bought from his clinic's boutique, worth more than most people's monthly salary. Her hair was perfectly styled, as always.

But something was wrong.

"I need you to understand something," she said, her voice trembling. "I didn't want to come here. I tried everything else. But nothing works."

"What are you talking about?"

She turned around.

Fang's breath caught in his throat.

The face was wrong. It was Mrs. Chen's face, yes. But it was also someone else's. Someone younger. Someone who had died in this very clinic three years ago.

The features were shifting.

Her left eye drooped, then tightened. Her nose bridge collapsed, then rebuilt itself. Her cheekbones rose and fell like waves on a stormy sea. And beneath it all, her skin rippled with a life of its own.

"I can feel it moving," she whispered. "Every day, it moves more. At first, I thought it was just swelling. But then I looked in the mirror, and I saw her."

"Who?"

"The girl. The one whose face you gave me."

Fang's blood ran cold. "That's impossible. The procedure is permanent."

"No." Mrs. Chen stepped closer, and he saw the tears streaming down her morphing face. "It's not permanent. It's just borrowed. And she wants it back."

She grabbed his hand and pressed it against her cheek. The skin was warm. Too warm. Alive. He felt something moving beneath it, like a snake writhing under silk.

"She talks to me at night," Mrs. Chen said. "She tells me what she's going to do. She's going to tear my face off from the inside. She's going to wear it until it rots. And then she's going to find you."

Fang pulled his hand away. "This is impossible. I've done hundreds of these procedures. Nothing like this has ever happened."

"Then why did it happen to me?"

He didn't have an answer.

The skin on her face rippled again, and for a split second, he saw the face beneath. The original face. The dead face. It stared at him with hollow eyes and a mouth that stretched into a grin.

"You need to remove it," Mrs. Chen said. "Tonight. Right now. I don't care what it costs."

"I can't."

"WHAT?"

He stepped back, raising his hands. "The procedure can't be reversed. The skin grafts bond with the host tissue. Removing them would destroy your face entirely."

"Then what am I supposed to do?"

Fang's mind raced. He'd never prepared for this. He'd never thought it would happen. The spirits were supposed to be trapped. Suppressed. He'd performed the rituals perfectly. He'd followed every step of the ancient texts.

But maybe the texts had been wrong.

Maybe the spirits had been waiting all along.

"Give me two days," he said. "I need to consult with someone. A specialist."

"What specialist?"

He didn't answer. He was already thinking of the Taoist. The one who had been asking questions. The one who might know how to undo what he'd done.

But if he went to her, he'd be admitting everything. He'd be handing her the evidence she needed to destroy him.

He had no choice.

"Stay here," he said. "Don't leave this room. Don't look in any mirrors. I'll be back."

Mrs. Chen grabbed his arm. Her grip was iron. "If you don't come back, I'll go to the police. I'll tell them everything."

"You won't."

"Why not?"

"Because if you do, they'll find out what you did. What you paid for. What you knew." He leaned in close, his voice dropping to a whisper. "And then they'll take your face away. And you'll be left with nothing."

She let go.

He walked out of the room, his heart pounding in his chest. The hallway stretched before him, endless and empty. He could feel the weight of the building above him, the weight of all those faces waiting in the dark.

The Taoist's shop was on the outskirts of the city. An hour's drive, maybe more. He didn't know if she'd be there. He didn't know if she'd help him.

But he had to try.

He was halfway to the elevator when his phone buzzed again.

A new message. From an unknown number.

*Dr. Fang.*

*We know what you keep in the basement.*

*We're coming for it.*

He stared at the screen, his blood turning to ice.

The elevator doors opened.

The Taoist stepped out.

She was shorter than he'd imagined. Older. Her face was lined with years of wisdom and hardship, her eyes sharp as knives. She wore a simple grey robe, and around her neck hung a jade pendant that glowed with a faint inner light.

"Dr. Fang," she said. "I was just about to visit you."

He looked from her to the phone in his hand. The message was gone. Vanished. As if it had never existed.

"Who else knows?" he asked.

She smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"Everyone who matters. And everyone who doesn't."

She stepped past him, toward the VIP room where Mrs. Chen was waiting.

He watched her go, too stunned to move.

The elevator doors closed behind her.

And in the basement, ten floors below, the first jar began to crack.

title: The Taoist Makes a House Call
content:
The scalpel felt wrong in his hand.

Dr. Fang Changwei had performed this procedure three hundred and forty-seven times. He knew the weight of the blade, the precise angle of entry, the way the dermal layer would separate from the subcutaneous fat like pages of a wet book. But tonight, the instrument trembled against his thumb.

He set it down.

“Is there a problem, Doctor Fang?”

The woman on the table was forty-three years old. Divorced. Two children in international school. Her pre-op photos showed the kind of face that had been pretty in her twenties, then slowly eroded by Shanghai's humidity and the quiet desperation of a husband who stopped coming home. She wanted her cheekbones back. She wanted the line of her jaw defined like it was in 2012. She wanted to look like the woman her daughter's piano teacher, who was twenty-six, currently looked like.

Fang smiled. His patient smile. The one that cost twelve years of medical school and three hundred thousand yuan in bribery to perfect.

“Minor delay, Mrs. Chen. I need to verify our inventory.”

The nurse, Xiao Li, followed him into the storage room. She was new. Two weeks in. Still had that eagerness that hadn't yet curdled into the hollow-eyed efficiency of the others.

“Doctor Fang, the preparation is complete. The skin is already in the solution.”

He didn't answer.

The refrigerator hummed against the tile wall. White. Commercial grade. Inside, arranged on titanium racks, were thirty-seven sealed containers. Each marked with a name. Mrs. Chen's was on the third shelf, second from the left.

He pulled it out.

The solution was clear. The skin inside floated like a silk scarf, impossibly thin, impossibly smooth. It shimmered under the fluorescent light. He had harvested it six days ago from a spirit that had been haunting a karaoke bar in Pudong. A young woman who died in 1998, still wearing her barista uniform, still smelling of cigarette smoke and jasmine tea. She had been easy to catch. The desperate ones always were.

But the skin in the container wasn't smooth anymore.

Fang leaned closer.

Small bumps dotted the surface. Like gooseflesh. Like pores opening.

“Doctor Fang?”

“Get out.”

Xiao Li's footsteps retreated. The door clicked shut.

He rotated the container under the light. The bumps were not growths. They were indentations. As if something from inside the skin was pushing outward. As if the face wanted to turn itself inside out.

His phone buzzed.

A patient. Asking about her recovery. He scrolled through the messages. The first one, from three weeks ago: *Dr. Fang, my skin feels tight. Is that normal?*

The second, from two weeks ago: *I think I see something moving under my cheek.*

The third, from yesterday: *The face in my mirror isn't mine anymore. It's someone else's. She's trying to smile but she doesn't know how.*

Fang deleted the messages. He always deleted the messages.

He returned to the operating room. Mrs. Chen was still sedated, her face slack, her breathing steady. The room smelled of antiseptic and something else. Something faintly sweet, like overripe fruit.

He picked up the scalpel.

The first incision went clean. The skin lifted away from the muscle. He worked quickly, methodically, his hands finding the rhythm that had made him famous. His clinic was booked six months in advance. Celebrities flew in from Beijing, from Hong Kong, from Singapore. They paid in cash. They never asked questions.

He placed the harvested skin in the solution. It would keep for three more weeks. Then he would sell it to a dealer in Shenzhen who would process it into injectables for the black market. The rich didn't need to know where the collagen came from. They just needed to look young.

Mrs. Chen's new face was ready.

He peeled the protective layer from the container. The spirit skin glistened. It was warm to the touch, even after refrigeration. He positioned it over her exposed features. The edges aligned perfectly. He pressed down.

The skin adhered.

For a moment, nothing happened. Then the face began to settle. The pores opened. The blood vessels connected. The cheeks filled with color. Mrs. Chen's wrinkles smoothed away. Her jawline sharpened. Her lips plumped.

She looked twenty-five again.

She would wake up in four hours, look in the mirror, and cry with joy. She would not know that the face she was crying over had belonged to a dead girl who never got to be twenty-five. She would not care.

Fang stripped off his gloves.

“Monitor her vitals. Call me if anything changes.”

Xiao Li nodded. Her eyes were fixed on Mrs. Chen's new face. Something flickered across her expression. Not awe. Not curiosity.

Recognition.

“Doctor Fang,” she said. “I know her.”

“You don't know her. She's under anesthetic.”

“No. The face. I know that face.”

Fang's hand stopped on the door handle.

“She was a customer at my brother's karaoke bar. He showed me the security footage from 1998. A girl came in alone, ordered jasmine tea, and never left. They found her body in the bathroom the next morning. Strangled.”

The room went cold.

Mrs. Chen's new face twitched.

“I remember because of her teeth,” Xiao Li continued. Her voice was flat. Mechanical. “She had a gap between her front teeth. Just like that.”

Fang looked at the operating table.

Mrs. Chen's lips parted.

The teeth inside were not hers. They were yellowed. Cracked. And between the front two, a narrow gap that hadn't been there when she was sedated.

“Get out,” Fang whispered.

“Doctor Fang—”

“GET OUT.”

Xiao Li ran.

Fang stood alone in the operating room. The lights hummed. The machines beeped. Mrs. Chen's new face stared up at the ceiling, perfectly still, perfectly beautiful.

Then it turned toward him.

The eyes did not open. The rest of the body remained motionless. But the face rotated on its own axis, the skin twisting, the muscles rearranging themselves beneath the surface. It was like watching a mask come alive.

“You took my skin.”

The voice came from Mrs. Chen's throat. But it was not her voice. It was higher. Younger. Laced with the static of an old recording.

“You took my skin, and now I'm inside her.”

Fang's hand found the scalpel on the tray.

“Let her go.”

“I can't. You glued me on.”

The face smiled. It was not a human smile. The lips stretched too wide. The skin around the mouth pulled taut, threatening to tear.

“But that's okay,” the face said. “Because she's going to walk out of here, and she's going to go home, and she's going to look in her mirror. And when she does, she's going to see me. She's going to see the girl you killed in the karaoke bar. She's going to see me staring back.”

Fang took a step forward.

“And I'm going to tell her everything.”

The door behind him opened.

“Doctor Fang.”

He turned. The Taoist stood in the doorway. Old man. Gray robes. A string of wooden beads wrapped around his wrist. He looked at Mrs. Chen's twisting face and did not flinch.

“I've been looking for you,” the Taoist said. “I think you have something that belongs to me.”

Fang's grip tightened on the scalpel.

“I don't know what you're talking about.”

“Oh, I think you do.” The Taoist stepped into the room. His beads clicked together. “Those spirits you've been skinning? They were my patients. I was supposed to guide them across. But someone kept stealing them. Someone kept cutting them open and wearing their faces.”

He stopped beside the operating table.

The face on Mrs. Chen stopped twisting. It looked up at the Taoist. And it wept.

Real tears. Salty. Human.

“Please,” the face whispered. “Please help me.”

The Taoist reached out. His fingers hovered over Mrs. Chen's cheek.

“I can't,” he said softly. “Not while you're attached to her. You're bound now. Blood to blood. Skin to skin.”

He turned to Fang.

“What have you done?”

Fang's phone buzzed again. Another message. Another patient.

He didn't need to read it. He already knew what it would say.

*Doctor Fang, the face in my mirror just blinked. But I didn't blink.*

The Taoist's eyes went hard.

“You have one hour to tell me where your collection is. Every jar. Every container. Every piece of skin you've stolen.”

“Or what?”

“Or I'll let them find you themselves.”

Mrs. Chen's new face laughed. It was a terrible sound. Wet and broken and hungry.

“He's right,” she said. “We're coming. All of us. And we want our faces back.”

The lights flickered.

The temperature dropped.

And somewhere in the basement of the clinic, in the refrigerator with the titanium racks, thirty-seven containers began to vibrate.
